qualifying_org_definition: Any nonprofit, faith-based organization, or government agency where the ABAWD performs volunteer/unpaid work. DC lists 'volunteer work' explicitly under the general work option (Option A: 80 hrs/month); no pre-approved host-site list or MOU is required for this lane. The separate SNAP Volunteer Program (Option B / Workfare) lane does require SNAP E&T enrollment and placement at an approved host site, but that is a distinct pathway with a lower hour threshold (hours = monthly SNAP benefit ÷ $17.95 DC minimum wage, typically ≤30 hrs/month).
verification_instrument: SNAP Verification of Employment or Qualifying Work Activity Form — released June 1, 2026. Filled out by the customer and signed by an employer or organization representative. No approved-site requirement for the general (Option A) lane. Separate SNAP Work Requirements Screening Form screens for exemptions at application/recertification.
hours_formula_detail: DC DHS states explicitly '80 hours per month (or 20 hours per week)' for the general work/volunteer lane (Option A). The SNAP Volunteer Program (Option B/Workfare) uses a different formula: monthly SNAP benefit ÷ $17.95 (DC minimum wage), typically yielding ≤30 hrs/month for most households. The 80-hour figure applies to the Tended-relevant general volunteer lane.
accepts_outofstate_detail: DC policy documents for Option A (general volunteer lane) describe qualifying work as any 'paid, unpaid, volunteer, or work done in exchange for goods and services' without any geographic restriction on where the organization is located. No DC DHS policy, SNAP E&T guidance, or advocacy materials reviewed address in-state vs. out-of-state nonprofit location. The federal default under 7 CFR 273.24 imposes no in-state requirement; absent a DC-specific restriction (none found), out-of-state nonprofits should be operationally accepted for Option A verification. Note: Option B (SNAP Volunteer Program / Workfare) requires placement at a DC DHS-approved host site list, which in practice would be local organizations, but that is a separate lane.
geographic_waivers: none — fully enforced statewide as of June 1, 2026. DC previously held a federal ABAWD waiver (insufficient-jobs basis) that was terminated by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA / H.R. 1), which eliminated that waiver authority for all states except those with unemployment above 10%. DC began enforcing ABAWD time limits on June 1, 2026.
submission_deadline: Within 10 days of the end of the month in which the change occurred (or within 10 days of the date the change happened if fewer than 10 days remain in the month). DC DHS SNAP Fact Sheet language: 'report within 10 days of the end of the month when the change happens.'
notes:
DC is assessed Tier 1 clean: official guidance from DC DHS and Legal Aid DC lists 'volunteer work' explicitly under the general work option (paid, unpaid, volunteer, or barter — 80 hrs/month), verified via the SNAP Verification of Employment or Qualifying Work Activity Form signed by an org representative, with no mandatory pre-step required. The SNAP Volunteer Program (workfare) is a separate, SNAP-E&T-administered lane that does require enrollment and agency-assigned placement hours. Confidence is medium rather than high because DC ABAWD enforcement only began June 1, 2026, published policy detail on the general volunteer lane mechanics is thin, and no authoritative DC policy manual or ACL equivalent was located online to confirm the absence of any hidden pre-step for independent volunteer reporting.

REFINEMENT PASS (2026-06-21): Adversarial research conducted targeting four angles the first pass did not cover: (1) SNAP E&T State Plan language; (2) mandatory work registration / employment development plan pre-steps; (3) approved-site list requirements; (4) Legal Aid DC and DC Hunger Solutions explainers for hidden gating. The DC DHS SNAP Volunteer Program one-pager (updated May 6, 2026) confirmed the two-option structure explicitly: Option A is the general work lane (paid, unpaid, volunteer, barter — 80 hrs/month, no SNAP E&T enrollment or approved-site requirement), and Option B is the SNAP Volunteer Program (Workfare), which does require SNAP E&T enrollment, a case manager assignment, and selection from an approved volunteer site list, but has a lower hour threshold (~30 hrs/month for most households). Three independent sources — DC DHS official pages, Legal Aid DC, and DC Hunger Solutions — all use identical language for Option A ('work (paid, unpaid, volunteer, or work done in exchange for goods and services)') without citing any mandatory pre-step or SNAP E&T enrollment for that lane. Work registration determination occurs at application/recertification by DHS, not as a separate recipient-initiated intake step before volunteer hours count. No mandatory employment development plan or case manager meeting was found for the general volunteer lane. The Tier 1 classification is confirmed. Confidence raised to HIGH: the general volunteer lane is now multi-source confirmed across DC DHS official materials and two front-line advocacy organizations, the two-lane structure (Option A vs. Option B/Workfare) is documented in primary source materials, and no hidden pre-step was found despite targeted adversarial search. Remaining unknown: remote_stance (DC has not published explicit guidance on whether remote/virtual volunteer work counts under Option A).

EXTENDED FIELDS PASS (2026-06-21): (1) Hours: 80 hrs/month confirmed federal_80 — DC DHS fact sheet and volunteer one-pager both state explicitly '80 hours per month (or 20 hours per week)' for Option A; Option B uses benefit÷$17.95 (workfare formula, ≤30 hrs for most) but that is a separate lane. (2) Out-of-state nonprofit: silent — no DC source addresses geographic restriction on certifying organizations under Option A; federal default (no restriction) applies; Option B approved-site list is local but irrelevant to the general lane. (3) Geographic waivers: none — DC's prior insufficient-jobs federal waiver was eliminated by OBBBA/H.R. 1; enforcement began June 1, 2026 with no current waivers; FNA waiver table corroborates no active DC waiver. (4) Submission UX: DC DHS fact sheet explicitly lists District Direct (online portal + mobile app), in-person service centers, mail, and fax as submission channels; reporting cadence is monthly; deadline is 10 days after end of the month the change occurred. (5) Form fields: the 'SNAP Verification of Employment or Qualifying Work Activity Form' is described only as 'filled out by the customer and signed by an employer or organization representative' — no published field list found; record carries the standard baseline field set.
